subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Jocelyn Bell Burnell and Pulsars
description: Learn Jocelyn Bell Burnell and Pulsars in one minute.

hook.text: A 24-year-old graduate student discovered pulsars in 1967. Her supervisor won the Nobel Prize instead.

buildup.text: Jocelyn Bell noticed a repeating signal in radio data—precise pulses every 1.3 seconds. She called it 'scruff.'

discovery.text: The signal came from a rapidly spinning neutron star emitting radiation beams—a pulsar. It was a new class of object.

twist.text: The 1974 Nobel Prize went to her supervisor Antony Hewish. The exclusion of Bell remains one of science's most debated snubs.

climax.text: In 2018, Bell won the Breakthrough Prize and donated the entire $3 million to fund underrepresented physics students.

punchline.text: She found pulsars. He got the Nobel. She donated millions to fix the system.

quiz.question: What did Jocelyn Bell Burnell discover in 1967?
options: Pulsars—rapidly spinning neutron stars emitting regular radio pulses
The first black hole candidate in the Milky Way
Gravitational waves from merging neutron stars
